introduction.p: In this section we will consider subspaces of general vector spaces.

In earlier sections, we studied vector spaces as entire collections of objects. In practice, however, we often work with smaller collections that live inside a larger vector space. In this activity, we explore when such a subset behaves like a vector space in its own right.
Rather than starting with a formal definition, you will examine examples and look for patterns that help explain which subsets work and which do not.

ol.li: All vectors of the form \begin{bmatrix}x \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}.
All vectors of the form \begin{bmatrix}x \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
All vectors of the form \begin{bmatrix}x \\ y \end{bmatrix} with x+y=0.
Decide whether adding two vectors from the subset produces another vector in the subset.
Decide whether multiplying a vector in the subset by a scalar keeps the result in the subset.
Identify one example where something fails, if it does.

statement.p: Now consider the following subsets of the vector space \mathbb{P}_2 of polynomials of degree at most 2.

ol.li: All polynomials with zero constant term.
All polynomials with constant term equal to 1.
All polynomials that have a fixed real number r as a root.
Decide whether the zero polynomial belongs to the subset.
Test whether the subset is closed under addition.
Test whether the subset is closed under scalar multiplication.

statement.p: In each of the previous examples, some subsets behaved well under the vector space operations, while others failed in specific ways.

ol.li: Which properties seem to fail most often?
Why does the failure of a single property prevent a subset from behaving like a vector space?
What role does the zero vector seem to play in all successful examples?

conclusion.p: These examples suggest that only certain subsets of a vector space are stable under the operations of addition and scalar multiplication. In the next section, we will formalize these observations by introducing the definition of a subspace.

url.visual: Subspaces are the Natural Subsets of Linear Algebra (6:25)
The Null Space and Column Space of a Matrix (10:40)

definition.p: Let V be a vector space over a field K. A subset W \subseteq V is said to be a subspace of V if the following conditions hold:

ol.li: \zerovec \in W, where \zerovec is the additive unit of V.
W is closed under addition: Whenever \uvec,\vvec \in W, then \uvec+\vvec \in W.
W is closed under scalar multiplication: Whenever k \in K and \uvec \in W, then k\uvec \in W.

example.p: As we have seen, the subspaces of \mathbb{R}^3 are:
Let V=\mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}}, the vector space of functions from real numbers to real numbers. Let W\subseteq V be the subset of continuous functions. Then W is a subspace of V.
Proof: We know from calculus that:

ol.li: the zero function, defined by f(x)=0 for all x, is continuous;
if f,g are continuous functions, then f+g is continuous;
if f is a continuous function and k a constant, then kf is continuous.

example.p: It follows that W contains \zerovec, and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. Therefore, W is a subspace of V.

Let V=\mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}}. Recall from calculus that a function f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} is called differentiable if the derivative f'(x) exists for all x\in\mathbb{R}. Let W\subseteq V be the subset of differentiable functions. Then W is a subspace of V.
We know from calculus that:

ol.li: the zero function, defined by f(x)=0 for all x, is differentiable. In fact, its derivative is f'(x)=0;
if f is a differentiable function and k a constant, then h=kf is differentiable. In fact, h'=kf'.

example.p: It follows that W contains \zerovec, and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. Therefore, W is a subspace of V.

Let V=\mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}, the vector space of sequences of real numbers. Let
W=\{a\in\mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}\mid \text{for all }n\ge 0,\ a_{n+2}=a_n+a_{n+1}\}.
In other words, W is the set of all sequences satisfying the recurrence relation a_{n+2}=a_n+a_{n+1}. Then W is a subspace of V.
Proof: Before we prove that W is a subspace, let us first consider an example. The following sequences are elements of W, because they both satisfy the recurrence:

Note that if we add these sequences, we get
a+b=(2,4,6,10,16,26,42,68,\ldots),
which again satisfies the recurrence. Therefore, the set W is closed under the addition of these particular sequences a and b.
We now prove the properties in general.

ol.li: Let z be the zero sequence, defined by z_n=0 for all n. Then z satisfies the recurrence relation, since for all n\ge 0, z_{n+2}=0=z_n+z_{n+1}. Therefore z\in W.

To show that W is closed under addition, consider any two sequences a,b\in W, and let c=a+b. Then for all n\ge 0,
c_{n+2}=a_{n+2}+b_{n+2} =(a_n+a_{n+1})+(b_n+b_{n+1}) =(a_n+b_n)+(a_{n+1}+b_{n+1}) =c_n+c_{n+1},
so c satisfies the recurrence. It follows that c\in W.

To show that W is closed under scalar multiplication, consider any k\in\mathbb{R} and a\in W, and let c=ka. Then for all n\ge 0,
c_{n+2}=ka_{n+2}=k(a_n+a_{n+1})=ka_n+ka_{n+1}=c_n+c_{n+1},
so c satisfies the recurrence. It follows that c\in W.

Let c denote the vector space of convergent, infinite sequences of real numbers. Let c_{0} denote the subset of infinite sequences of real numbers that converge to 0. Show that c_{0} is a subspace of c.

Let W denote the subset of c (the vector space of convergent, infinite sequences) that consists of those sequences having finite support. By that, we mean that W consists of sequences where only finitely many of the terms are nonzero. Is W a subspace of c? Why or why not?
Is the set of convergent, geometric sequences (i.e. those of the form a_{n}=ar^{n} where |r|<1) a subspace of c? Why or why not?
Is the set \displaystyle S=\left\{ (a_{n}) \in c : \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty}a_{n}= 1 \right\} a subspace of c? Why or why not?
Is the set of all constant sequences a subspace of c? Why or why not?

We show that if (\avec_{n}) and (\bvec_{n}) are sequences in c_{0}, then their sum is as well. But the sum of two sequences that converge to 0 also converges to 0. We also show that if (\avec_{n}) is a sequence converging to 0 then any multiple of it will also converge to 0. But this is clear. So c_{0} is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. The zero element is obviously the sequence of all 0's and hence clearly converges to 0 and is in c_{0}.

W is a subspace. If we add two such sequences, then the sum will only have a finite number of terms that are not zero (at most double the number of nonzero terms in each individual sequence). If we multiply such a sequence by a constant, it will have (at most) the same number of nonzero terms. The zero sequence (consisting of all 0's) has no nonzero terms and so is also in W.
No. Adding the sequences (r,r^{2},r^{3},\hdots) and (s,s^{2},s^{3},\hdots) gives a sequence (r+s, r^{2}+s^{2}, r^{3}+s^{3},\hdots) which is not geometric unless either r or s is 0.
No. The constant sequence (1,1,1,\hdots) is in S, but adding this sequence to itself will not yield a sequence in S.

Yes. The zero sequence (0,0,0,\hdots) is the zero element and is constant. Adding two constant sequences will yield a constant sequence. Multiplying each term of a sequence by a scalar k will produce another constant sequence.

example.p: Let V=\mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}}. Recall from calculus that a differential equation is an equation about an unknown function and its derivatives. For example f''=-f is a differential equation. The functions f(x)=\sin x, f(x)=\cos x, and f(x)=0 are examples of solutions of this differential equation. Let W be the set of all functions that are solutions of the differential equation f''=-f. Then W is a subspace of V.
Proof:The zero function f(x)=0 is a solution of the differential equation, and therefore an element of W.
To show that W is closed under addition, let f,g\in W and consider h=f+g. Then f''=-f and g''=-g, and therefore h''=f''+g''=-f+(-g)=-h. Therefore h\in W.
To show that W is closed under scalar multiplication, let k\in\mathbb{R} and f\in W, and consider h=kf. Then f''=-f, and therefore h''=kf''=k(-f)=-h. It follows that h\in W.

Consider \mathbb{P}_2, the vector space of polynomials of degree at most 2, with real coefficients. Fix some real number r, and let W\subseteq \mathbb{P}_2 be the subset of polynomials that have r as a root. Then W is a subspace of \mathbb{P}_2.

Proof: We can express W as follows:
W=\{p\in \mathbb{P}_2\mid p(r)=0\}.
We need to show that W is a subspace.
1. The zero polynomial, given by 0(x)=0, satisfies 0(r)=0, so 0\in W.
2. To show that W is closed under addition, assume p,q\in W, and let s=p+q. Then p(r)=0 and q(r)=0, therefore s(r)=p(r)+q(r)=0. It follows that s\in W.
3. To show that W is closed under scalar multiplication, assume p(x)\in W and k be a scalar. Then (kp)(r)=k(p(r))=k0=0, and therefore kp\in W.
Let V be an arbitrary vector space over a field K. Then \{\zerovec\} is a subspace of V, called the zero subspace. Also, V is a subspace of itself.

Proof: Clearly \{\zerovec\} contains \zerovec, and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication because \zerovec+\zerovec=\zerovec and k\zerovec=\zerovec for all k. Similarly, V contains \zerovec and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, because addition and scalar multiplication are operations on V. Therefore, both \{\zerovec\} and V are subspaces of V.

li.p: Let V=C(-\infty,\infty), the vector space of continuous real-valued functions. Define W to be the set of functions \fvec \in V for which \fvec' - \fvec = 0. For example, \fvec(x)=e^{x} is in W. Show that W is a subspace of V.

Let V=C(-\infty,\infty) as before. Define W to be the set of functions \fvec \in V for which \fvec'' + \fvec = 0. For example, \fvec(x)=\sin x and \fvec(x) = \cos x are in W. Is W a subspace of V? If so, prove it. If not, why not?

Let V = \Mvec_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}), the vector space of 2\times 2 real-valued matrices. Is W = \left\{ \left[ \begin{array}{cc}a & 0 \\ 0 & b\end{array} \right] : a,b \in \mathbb{R}\right\} a subspace of V? If so, prove it. If not, why not? If so, what is the dimension of this subspace?

Let V = \Mvec_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}), the vector space of 2\times 2 real-valued matrices. Is W = \left\{ \left[ \begin{array}{cc}a & b \\ 0 & a\end{array} \right] : a,b \in \mathbb{R}\right\} a subspace of V? If so, prove it. If not, why not? If so, what is the dimension of this subspace?

Let V = \Mvec_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}). Show that W = \left\{ A: A\left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1\end{array} \right]A \right\} is a subspace of V.
Let V = \mathbb{P}_{2}, the vector space of polynomials with real coefficients of degree at most 2. Let W = \left\{ p \in V : p(0)=1\right\}. Is W a subspace of V? If so, prove it. If not, why not?

Let V = \mathbb{P}_{2}, the vector space of polynomials with real coefficients of degree at most 2. Let W = \left\{ p(x)=ax^{2}+bx+c \mid a+b+c=0\right\}. Is W a subspace of V? If so, prove it. If not, why not?

Let V=C(-\infty,\infty), the vector space of continuous real-valued functions. Define W = C_{0}(-\infty,\infty), the set of functions \fvec \in V that vanish at infinity. That is \displaystyle W = \left\{ \fvec \in V : \lim_{x \rightarrow \pm\infty}\fvec(x) = 0 \right\}. Is W a subspace of V? Why or why not?

The zero vector, {\bfseries{0}}(x) = 0 (the constant zero function) is in W since it satisfies the equation \fvec' - \fvec=0. If \fvec and \gvec both satisfy the equation, then \fvec+\gvec also satisfies the equation since (\fvec+\gvec)' - (\fvec+\gvec) = (\fvec' - \fvec) + (\gvec' - \gvec) = 0 +0 = 0. Also, if \fvec \in W, and k is any real number, then k\fvec \in W since (k\fvec)' - (k\fvec) = k(\fvec' - \fvec) = k(0) = 0.

W is a subspace. The zero vector, {\bfseries{0}}(x) = 0 (the constant zero function) is in W since it satisfies the equation \fvec'' + \fvec=0. If \fvec and \gvec both satisfy the equation, then \fvec+\gvec also satisfies the equation since (\fvec+\gvec)'' + (\fvec+\gvec) = (\fvec'' + \fvec) + (\gvec'' + \gvec) = 0 +0 = 0. Also, if \fvec \in W, and k is any real number, then k\fvec \in W since (k\fvec)'' + (k\fvec) = k(\fvec'' + \fvec) = k(0) = 0.

Yes and the dimension is 2. The zero matrix {\zerovec}= \left[ \begin{array}{cc}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array} \right] is clearly in W. If A=\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1 & 0 \\ 0 & b_1\end{array} \right] \in W and B=\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_2 & 0 \\ 0 & b_2\end{array} \right] \in W, then A+B =\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1+a_2 & 0 \\ 0 & b_1+b_2\end{array} \right] which is clearly in W as well. Also, for any scalar k, we have kA = k \left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1 & 0 \\ 0 & b_1\end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}ka_1 & 0 \\ 0 & kb_1\end{array} \right] which is also clearly in W. A basis is \left\{ \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array} \right], \left[ \begin{array}{cc}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array} \right] \right\}.

Yes and the dimension is 2. The zero matrix {\zerovec}= \left[ \begin{array}{cc}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array} \right] is clearly in W. If A=\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1 & b_1 \\ 0 & a_1\end{array} \right] \in W and B=\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_2 & b_2 \\ 0 & a_2\end{array} \right] \in W, then A+B =\left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1+a_2 & b_1+b_2 \\ 0 & a_1+a_2\end{array} \right] which is clearly in W as well. Also, for any scalar k, we have kA = k \left[ \begin{array}{cc}a_1 & b_1 \\ 0 & a_1\end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}ka_1 & kb_1 \\ 0 & ka_1\end{array} \right] which is also clearly in W. A basis is \left\{ \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array} \right], \left[ \begin{array}{cc}0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0\end{array} \right] \right\}.

Since the \zerovec matrix commutes with any matrix, we know that {\zerovec}\in W. Suppose A, B \in W. Then A\left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1\end{array} \right]A and B\left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1\end{array} \right]B. So (A+B)[ 1 & 0 1 & -1 ] & \amp = & A[ 1 & 0 1 & -1 ] + B[ 1 & 0 1 & -1 ] & \amp = & [ 1 & 0 1 & -1 ]A + [ 1 & 0 1 & -1 ]B & \amp = & [ 1 & 1 0 & -1 ](A+B).
Also, (kA)\left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right] = k \left( A\left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right] \right) = k\left( \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right]A \right) = \left[ \begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1\end{array} \right](kA).
No. The zero polynomial is not in W.

Yes. First, the zero polynomial {\zerovec(x) = 0 is in W since all coefficients are 0 (and hence their sum is too). If \pvec, \qvec \in W, where \pvec(x) = a_{1}x^{2} + b_{1}x + c_{1} and \qvec(x) = a_{2}x^{2} + b_{2}x + c_{2}, then (\pvec+\qvec)(x) = a_{1}x^{2} + b_{1}x + c_{1} + a_{2}x^{2} + b_{2}x + c_{2} = (a_{1}+a_{2})x^{2} + (b_{1}+b_{2})x + (c_{1}+c_{2}) and we have (a_{1}+a_{2}) + (b_{1}+b_{2}) + (c_{1}+c_{2})=0 since a_{1}+b_{1}+c_{1}=0 and a_{2}+b_{2}+c_{2}=0. Also, (k\pvec)(x) = k(a_{1}x^{2}+b_{1}x+c_{1}) = (ka_{1})x^{2} + (kb_{1})x + (kc_{1}) where ka_{1} + kb_{1} + kc_{1} = k(a_{1}+b_{1}+c_{1})=0 so that W is closed under addition and scalar multiplication.

Yes. The zero function {\zerovec}(x) = 0 is in W since it vanishes at \pm \infty. Adding two functions that vanish at \pm \infty will produce a function that also vanishes there. And multiplying such a function by a constant will still vanish at \pm \infty.

subsection.title: Subspaces are vector spaces
subsection.p: The interest of subspaces lies in the fact that they are vector spaces in their own right, as stated in the following proposition.

statement.p: Let W be a subspace of a vector space V. Then W satisfies the vector space axioms (A1)–(A4) and (SM1)–(SM4), with respect to the same operations (addition and scalar multiplication) as those defined on V.

proof.p: Since W is a subspace, it is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. This ensures that addition and scalar multiplication are well-defined operations on W. The axioms (A1), (A2), (A4), and (SM1)–(SM4) all obviously hold in W, because they hold in V (two elements of W are equal in W if and only if they are equal in V). The axiom (A3) holds because \zerovec\in W.

statement.p: Let V be a vector space over some field K, and consider a set of vectors S\subseteq V. Then \mathrm{span}(S) is the smallest subspace of V containing S. More explicitly, we have:

ol.li: The set \mathrm{span}(S) is a subspace of V, and S\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S).
If W is any other subspace of V such that S\subseteq W, then \mathrm{span}(S)\subseteq W.

To show that \mathrm{span}(S) is a subspace, first note that \zerovec\in\mathrm{span}(S), because \zerovec is the empty linear combination. Also, if \vvec,\uvec\in\mathrm{span}(S), then by definition of span, there exist
\vvec_1,\ldots,\vvec_k,\uvec_1,\ldots,\uvec_\ell\in S and a_1,\ldots,a_k,b_1,\ldots,b_\ell\in K such that
\vvec \amp =a_1\vvec_1+\cdots+a_k\vvec_k, \uvec \amp =b_1\uvec_1+\cdots+b_\ell \uvec_\ell.
Then
\vvec+\uvec=a_1\vvec_1+\cdots+a_k\vvec_k+b_1\uvec_1+\cdots+b_\ell \uvec_\ell,
and therefore \vvec+\uvec\in\mathrm{span}(S). It follows that \mathrm{span}(S) is closed under addition. The proof for scalar multiplication is similar. Finally, every \vvec\in S is trivially a linear combination of itself, \vvec=1\vvec, and therefore S\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S).

Consider any other subspace W of V such that S\subseteq W. To show that \mathrm{span}(S)\subseteq W, consider an arbitrary element \vvec\in\mathrm{span}(S). By definition of span, there exist \vvec_1,\ldots,\vvec_k\in S and a_1,\ldots,a_k\in K such that
\vvec=a_1\vvec_1+\cdots+a_k\vvec_k.
By assumption, \vvec_1,\ldots,\vvec_k\in W. Since W is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, it follows that \vvec\in W. Since \vvec was an arbitrary element of \mathrm{span}(S), it follows that \mathrm{span}(S)\subseteq W.

subsection.p: While the last proposition looks technical, it can actually be useful for proving that two sets of vectors span the same subspace. The following in an example of this.

example.p: To show that two sets are equal, we must show that each is a subset of the other. So we will show \mathrm{span}(S)\subseteq\mathrm{span}(T) and \mathrm{span}(T)\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S). By , it is sufficient to show that S\subseteq\mathrm{span}(T) and T\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S), i.e., we must show that every element of S is a linear combination of elements of T and vice versa.

1. S\subseteq\mathrm{span}(T). We have
x^2-2x+1 \amp =(-1)(x^2-1)+2(x^2-x)\in\mathrm{span}(T), x-1 \amp =1(x^2-1)-1(x^2-x)\in\mathrm{span}(T).
Since each element of S is an element of \mathrm{span}(T), it follows that S\subseteq\mathrm{span}(T). By , this implies that \mathrm{span}(S)\subseteq\mathrm{span}(T).

2. T\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S). We have
x^2-1\amp =1(x^2-2x+1)-2(x-1)\in\mathrm{span}(S), x^2-x\amp =1(x^2-2x+1)-1(x-1)\in\mathrm{span}(S).
Since each element of T is an element of \mathrm{span}(S), it follows that T\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S). By , this implies that \mathrm{span}(T)\subseteq\mathrm{span}(S).

ul.li: A subspace is a subset of a vector space that contains the zero vector and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication.
Many familiar collections, such as solution sets of linear equations, spaces of functions with special properties, and sets defined by linear conditions, naturally form subspaces.
Every subspace is itself a vector space when equipped with the same operations as the larger space.
The span of a set of vectors is the smallest subspace containing that set, providing a systematic way to describe subspaces.

statement.p: Determine whether the subset of a vector space, together with the usual vector addition and scalar multiplication, is a subspace of the given vector space.

match.premise: The set of all vectors of the form (x,0)\in\mathbb{R}^2.
The set of all vectors (x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^2 such that x+y=0.
The set of all polynomials in \mathbb{P}_2 with zero constant term.
The set of all vectors (x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^2 such that x+y=1.
The set of all polynomials in \mathbb{P}_2 of degree exactly 2.

statement.p: Each card describes a property of a subset of a vector space. Match each property with one conclusion that follows.

match.premise: A nonempty subset is closed under addition and scalar multiplication.
A subset contains the zero vector and is closed under addition.

response.p: Scalar closure must still be checked

match.premise: A subset contains the zero vector and is closed under scalar multiplication.

response.p: Additive closure must still be checked

match.premise: A subset is the span of a set of vectors.

response.p: The subset is automatically a subspace
